---
title: "1.7 APIs and Libraries"
subtitle: "Part 2: Turtle Library and Dot Operator"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Turtle as an API example

The `Turtle` Java library, written by one of the authors of CSAwesome2, Dr. Barbara Ericson, allows you to create drawings using an animated turtle that moves around the screen.

The turtle can move forward, turn, and draw lines.

The turtle library is a collection of classes that can be imported into your code and is built in to this e-book.

## Classes

### Building blocks of Java

<span class="term">Classes</span> are the building blocks of Java and object-oriented programming.

A class defines a specific reference type.

Programmers often create new classes like `Turtle` so that it can be used as a new data type.

Classes like `Turtle` and class libraries can be utilized to create objects like a particular turtle named `yertle`.

## Data and Behavior

### What a class defines

A class defines the data and behavior for all objects of that type.

![Turtle attributes and behaviors](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-7-application-program-interface-and-libraries/turtleOOD.png){fig-align="center" width="58%"}

The picture shows some Turtle <span class="term">attributes</span> like `name`, `width`, `height`, and `color`, and methods like `forward()` and `backward()`.

## Attributes and Behaviors

### Documentation vocabulary

<span class="term">Attributes</span> (属性), sometimes called <span class="term">fields</span> in documentation, refer to the data related to the class and are stored in variables.

<span class="term">Behaviors</span> (行为) refer to what objects of the class can do or what can be done with it, and are defined by methods.

The `Turtle` class has attributes like `name`, `height`, and `width`, and behaviors/methods like `forward()` and `turnLeft()`.

## Reading Documentation

### Parentheses identify methods

How can you tell attributes and methods apart when you look at library documentation?

One easy way is that methods will always have parentheses after them, for example `forward()` or `println()`.

Sometimes these parentheses are empty, but still necessary, and sometimes they contain data that the method needs to do its job.

Attributes are variables and will not have parentheses after them.

## Dot Operator

### Run an object's methods

The Java program in this topic creates a `Turtle` object called `yertle` using the `Turtle` class.

The following code tells the turtle to move forward and then turn right.

```java
yertle.forward();
yertle.turnRight();
```

The <span class="term">dot operator</span> `.` is used to run an object's methods, just like in `System.out.println()`.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: TurtleAPI`

Textbook prompt: Try clicking the run button below to see what the following program does.

Then add 1 more line of code on line 16 to make `yertle` go `forward()` again.

## Starter Code

### `activecode:: TurtleAPI`

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtleTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // Do not change the next 2 lines
        World habitat = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);

        // Code to make yertle move forward and turn right
        yertle.forward();
        yertle.turnRight();
        // Add a line of code to make yertle go forward() again

        // Do not change
        habitat.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: TurtleAPI`

Runestone checks that the code contains at least two occurrences of:

```java
yertle.forward
```

Expected result label: `yertle.forward() count` should be `2+`.

The starter code already has one `yertle.forward();`, so the student must add one more.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: turtle_dir`

Which way does a turtle face when it is first created?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. North
- B. South
- C. East
- D. West
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">A. North</span>

Turtles start off facing north, which is toward the top of the page.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: turtle_type`

What type of thing is `Turtle` in the program above?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. class
- B. object
- C. attribute
- D. method
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">A. class</span>

`Turtle` is a class that defines the data and behaviors for all turtles.

## Answer Reasoning

### `turtle_type`

::: {.tight-list}
- `yertle` is an object of type `Turtle`.
- An attribute is something the object knows about itself.
- A method is something an object can do, like go forward.
:::

Use these distinctions when reading API documentation.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: type_turn_right`

What type of thing is `turnRight` in the program above?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. object
- B. class
- C. attribute
- D. method
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">D. method</span>

A behavior or method is something an object can do, like turn right.

## Answer Reasoning

### `type_turn_right`

::: {.tight-list}
- An object has data and behavior.
- A class defines the data and behavior for all objects of that type.
- An attribute is something the object knows about itself.
:::

`turnRight` is a behavior, so it is a method.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: type_position`

What type of thing is the position of a turtle in a world?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. object
- B. class
- C. attribute
- D. method
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">C. attribute</span>

An attribute is something the object knows about itself, like its position.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: attributes_class`

What are attributes of a class?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. Methods that perform tasks
- B. Data related to the class stored in variables
- C. The arguments of a class
- D. Packages that contain the class
:::

Correct answer: <span class="mark">B. Data related to the class stored in variables</span>

Attributes are not methods, arguments, or packages.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that `Turtle` is a class that can be used as a new reference type
- identify `yertle` as an object created from the `Turtle` class
- distinguish attributes/fields from behaviors/methods
- use parentheses to tell methods apart from attributes in documentation
- explain the dot operator in `yertle.forward()` and `System.out.println()`
- preserve the `TurtleAPI` starter and add only one more `yertle.forward();`
:::

## End

### Continue to Turtle practice

The next deck keeps the mixed-up code task and Turtle Drawing groupwork challenge.
